Although a potential contamination could have a significant impact on production, the likelihood that this has occurred is assessed as low.

Non Conformance Title: Freezing date not recorded on CoA
Classification justification: The lack of including freezing date is a loss in traceability for the batch documentation and CoA and is a direct omittance from the information specified to be included by the product specification. However, as the product quality is unaffected and the freezing date can be strongly indicated by the signed date of the other processes during batch release it is considered to be a low risk classification.

Non Conformance Title: Leakage from harvest bag during xcellerator B9325 run
Classification justification: The leakage was minor (~10 mL) and contained within a secondary bag inside the Xcellerator refrigerator. There was continuous outflow from the system, minimizing the risk of contamination. No abnormal parameters or signs of contamination were observed.

Non Conformance Title: Document templates not having revision workflow
Classification justification: This non-conformance regards the potential lack of traceability during update of the document templates, but does not pose any direct impact to products, productions, or HSE.

Non Conformance Title: Delayed bi-annual cleaning of cleanroom
Classification justification: This nonconformance is classified as low risk because daily, weekly, and monthly cleaning routines were consistently maintained, and environmental monitoring results showed no deviations or concerns. The delay in bi-annual cleaning did not impact cleanroom integrity or ongoing production.

Non Conformance Title: Foreign object observed in CL1000 unit
Investigation summary: After 13 days in quarantine, no infection was observed in CL1000 B9404, indicating the foreign object was sterile. This suggests it likely originated from a sterilized material - probably the flask itself, based on previous NCs registered - rather than from operator contamination.
Impact Assessment: This NC resulted in loss of crude harvest  from one CL1000, and hence some final product, for project 2025-35. However, the remaining CL1000s of the project are expected to produce enough to comply with the customer agreement.  Impact is therefore considered low.
Root cause description: Based on the investigation and other similar NCs, the foreign object in B9404 likely originated from the CL1000 flask itself. It is possibly a thin sliver shed from the black rubber membrane during manufacturing, rather than from operator contamination or the ADCF medium.
Classification justification: While foreign objects have previously been observed in CL1000s (ref. NC360 & NC408 in the last 12 months), these were round or speck-like in appearance. In contrast, the foreign object identified in B9404 is hair-like, which significantly increases the contamination risk.

Non Conformance Title: Temperature sensor cable discarded after Xcellerator run termination
Classification justification: No impact on product quality or validated state of equipment/process. Also no impact on employee safety or on customer agreements. The only possible impact could have been economic, if a new temperature sensor had to be purchased for about 10.000 NOK, but this was not necessary as we had a spare one available.

Non Conformance Title: Rejected batch used as reference for QC
Classification justification: There is no impact on final product quality. The QC test E6706 was invalidated and the analysis repeated using a valid reference (E6707). All impacted batches met approved specifications. The IPC analysis are used for monitoring and estimation, not formal batch disposition. Since production outcomes were successful there is no impact to any production timelines.

Non Conformance Title: More product shipped to Abbott Ireland than ordered
Classification justification: This NC does not pose a risk to product quality and product performance. Potentially, if re-occurring such NC could negatively affect customer satisfaction and potentially business continuity, but this event was resolved quickly in agreement with the customer.  In this case, the outcome did not result in financial loss, instead the customer accepted and paid for the additional product.

Non Conformance Title: CEDEX Run numbers used twice for different analyses
Classification justification: No impact on product quality, validated state of equipment/process, customer agreements or employee safety.

Non Conformance Title: Incorrect date recorded for correction of documented data
Classification justification: This NC does not impact any validated state of process or equipment. It does not impact the product quality, customer agreement or employee safety.

Non Conformance Title: Liquid in pipette
Classification justification: The pipette was not used for any QC analysis recently as seen in batch documentation. Any IPC analysis is purely for getting an idea of how the production is going and does not impact the actual quality of the product. Since there is no impact to the product or otherwise timeline of any production it is considered to be a low risk NC.

Non Conformance Title: NC 1. Internal audit: customer handling and realization of products.
Classification justification: Minor Deviation according to ISO 9001: 2015. 7.3 (awareness) and 5.2 (management)

Non Conformance Title: NC 2. Internal audit: Customer handling and realization of products
Classification justification: Minor NC according to ISO 9001:2015. Only one product was undergoing dialysis at the time, and the dialysis documentation was correctly completed. Therefore, there was no possibility of product mix-up, placement in an incorrect buffer, or insufficient dialysis

Non Conformance Title: Lost data on DIA169 temp logger due to low battery
Classification justification: Based on the stable temperature performance before and after the deviation, and the absence of similar issues in other incubators, the likelihood of a systemic failure or ongoing equipment malfunction is considered low. No product or sample impact is expected given the short duration and limited extent of the deviation. The incubator has demonstrated consistent operational performance and power loss in Sirius loggers are not a common issue, supporting the conclusion that the risk associated with this event is minimal.

Non Conformance Title: Infection in production
Investigation summary: The contamination was first observed in one flask during the expansion phase and subsequently spread to other flasks over two weeks. A review of handling procedures, environmental conditions, equipment functionality, and materials revealed no deviations or systemic issues. The contamination pattern suggests either cross-contamination during early manipulations or latent contamination introduced before freezing the Master Cell Bank (MCB). No evidence of cleanroom breach or equipment failure was found.
Impact Assessment: The non-conformance resulted in contamination of 2D2 cell cultures during the expansion phase, preventing timely inoculation into the maximizer. As a consequence, the production timeline was delayed. Since we could not risk inoculating into the maximizer with potentially compromised cells, production was restarted by Thawing new cells on the 1st December 2025. Fresh, uncontaminatyed cells were inoculated into CL1000 reactors. No product was released outside of specification, and no safety risk was identified. However, the delay impacted project scheduling, cost of inoculating CL1000s, and required additional production efforts.   Product/Batch Impact: The contaminated flasks were contained      and discarded.    Production Use: Fresh, uncontaminated cells      were used to inoculate CL1000 on 01st December 2025. No      contaminated material entered production.    Customer      Impact:        Schedule: Current estimate is on time based       on re‑start in CL1000.      Delivery:       [HAR VI ESTIMERT DELIVERY?]      Communication: [[ER KUNDEN       NOTIFIED?]
Root cause description: 5 Whys Root‑Cause Analysis 1. Why did flasks become contaminated? Microorganisms entered during open handling (thaw/expansion/passaging). 2. Why could microbes enter during open handling? Aseptic field was likely disrupted (e.g., transient breach in technique, traffic/interruption, tool or glove contact outside first air, or re‑use of an item between flasks). 3. Why was the aseptic field disrupted? High workload / sequential handling of multiple flasks increased touchpoints, possible time pressure, seasonal operator factors (flu season) may increase shedding risk even with masks. 4. Why did the disruption lead to staggered contamination? Cross‑contamination at later manipulations created delayed onset, also, low starting bioburden can yield longer lag phases → visible contamination at different times. 5. Why is this event recurring 3–5×/year? The process design is inherently open, with multiple manual steps, although mitigated, residual risk remains and is accepted within controlled limits. Primary Root Cause (most probable): Aseptic breach during an open handling step (thaw/expansion/passaging) resulting in introduction of microorganisms into one flask and subsequent cross‑contamination into additional flasks during later manipulations. Contributing Causes:        Open process design (inherent residual      risk, occurs 3–5× per year).   Sequential handling of multiple flasks increases      cross‑contamination risk.   Seasonal factors (flu season) raising bioburden      pressure despite compliance.   Incubator traffic/shared use during the period (if      applicable).   Detection limits of routine visual/viability      checks (see Testing Limitations).
Classification justification: Cells had to be discarded after three cycles of infection. Production deadline might not be met. Changed production to CL1000 instead of Maximizer

Non Conformance Title: Incubator door left open over night
Classification justification: Despite CO₂ deviation (0.7% for ~19 hours), temperature remained stable (35–37°C). Post-incident checks confirmed that the cells did not exhibit any signs of atypical growth or compromised viability. After inoculation, the cells appear to produce as expected.Therefore, this incident is not considered to have an impact on product quality or process integrity.

Non Conformance Title: Tank Leakage
Classification justification: A known or potential product quality/safety effect.  A significant incompliance issue with customer agreements (e.g.  Quality Agreement, Product Specification etc.).  Financial loss,  Production delayed due to shortage of medium.  Risk of infection if used in production.

Non Conformance Title: Error in worksheet after dividing the Maximizer SOP into seperate SOPs with new associated worksheets.
Classification justification: No impact on process or product quality, customer agreements or employee safety.

Non Conformance Title: CAPA SOP Effectiveness Evaluation Requirements Not Fully Defined
Investigation summary: A 5 Whys investigation determined that the CAPA SOP allows effectiveness evaluation to be optional by exempting preventive actions and assigning QA discretion generally for all CAPAs. This resulted from a legacy interpretation of ISO 9001:2015 clause 10.2 and earlier preventive action concepts, introduced to provide flexibility and reduce administrative burden. The broader ISO 9001:2015 intent that actions addressing risks and undesired effects should be effective was therefore not fully reflected. The gap was identified during a planned SOP update, with no prior quality signals triggering reassessment. This gap reflects a governance clarification opportunity rather than evidence of CAPA ineffectiveness.  The change to the SOP was applicable from SOP5039 from version 4.0. through CMID54 and CAPA150. Historical CAPAs were approved under the applicable procedure at the time, and no recurrence or quality signals have been observed that would warrant retrospective review. The QMS ensures that any recurrence or trend triggers new investigation and action. Corrective action will involve update of SOP5039 and its associated WIN, followed by and update to the Bizzmine electronic workflow to close this gap. During the transition period, open CAPAs will be managed per the current SOP. Where practicable, QA may request effectiveness evaluation consistent with the revised approach, however, mandatory application will begin upon SOP effective date.
Impact Assessment: Review of quality system indicators shows:    no recurrence of nonconformities, adverse trends, customer complaints, audit findings, or other quality signals that would suggest impact to product or service quality, patient safety, or regulatory compliance. The discrepancy was identified proactively during a planned SOP update, and existing QMS mechanisms remain effective in detecting and addressing any ineffective actions. Therefore, the overall impact is assessed as low.
Root cause description: Root Cause (derived from 5 Whys): A legacy interpretation of CAPA governance prioritised flexibility by allowing effectiveness evaluation to be discretionary, rather than establishing effectiveness as a mandatory, risk-based requirement aligned with ISO 9001:2015 intent.
Classification justification: the issue is limited to documentation and governance clarity, with no evidence of ineffective CAPAs, recurrence, or impact to product or customer quality, and was identified proactively during routine system improvement.

Non Conformance Title: Non-conformance ID 429 having mismatch between systemID and customID
Investigation summary: Error with the database caused a mismatch between the customID and systemID fields of the NC collection. The customID have been replaced throughout the platform with systemID for consistent traceability. Service person have prevented this from happening through options within the SQL server.
Impact Assessment: Potential mismatch between referenced NCID after NC428 in documents. The NCID will match systemID after NC441.
Root cause description: Two fold:  Mismatch between IDs: - Root cause is having two different identifiers that are generated in similar but subtly different ways thus allowing one to drift in response to database error. Random skip/drift: - Root cause is the identity cache in the SQL server registering an extra" ID during the registration of an NC during internet issues or similar computer lag"
Classification justification: Missing ID numbers with no trace of existence or documentation related to them will affect the integrity of the QMS. Potential impact on the the validation status of the NC process collection in BizzMine. It is crucial to have control over quality documents and records for securing compliance and to make sure no records or information are lost.  The software used to manage documents and records is validated to be robust with no possibility for users to delete information permanently.  That NC ID numbers are missing with no record of them is thus considered to be a high risk.

Non Conformance Title: Leakage from harvest tube in Maximizer
Investigation summary: Clamp was not opened before harvest startet, resulting in a gradual pressure buildup that caused leakage. The extended time between fill/flush and inoculation contributed to the event. Usually inoculation will be performed within day 50, this have not been an issue before. After this incident and after the change of the tube there has been no change in the results from the pH and glucose mesurements.
Impact Assessment: No impact to product quality identified, as no product was present in the system at the time of the event. The deviation occurred prior to inoculation. However, the incident resulted in bioreactor deformation. As no contamination was observed over time and the system was ready for use when needed this non-conformance has no impact on product quality or timelines.
Root cause description: The root cause was a gap in the instructions regarding connecting the bottle and opening the clamp, as the time in the system was considered sufficient from Fill/flush to inoculation and harvesting.
Classification justification: This NC is classified as Low Risk because the event occurred during the fill/flush stage, prior to inoculation. Consequently, there was no impact on product quality as no biological material was present in the system. While a mechanical failure occurred (tubing deformation), the immediate corrective actions of tubing replacement (as per suggestions by the service provider) and a robust disinfection protocol, successfully restored the system to its validated state without affecting the production timeline or incurring significant financial loss. Verification through pH, glucose, and microbial sampling confirmed the system remained fit for use.

Non Conformance Title: Leakage in CL1000
Classification justification: The leakage occurred at Harvest 7, within the supplier-assured safe timeframe for CL1000 units. This non-conformance is classified as low risk, as the leakage was between compartments and did not impact product quality.

Non Conformance Title: Cells thawed without additive
Investigation summary: Based on the PS, the operator who performed Thaw991 understood the requirement of addition of HFCS to apply only to the initial thawing of the customer‑supplied vial used to generate the PSS. Based on this interpretation, the operator did not add HFCS to Thaw991, which was a QC thawing of the PSS.  Several QC ELISAs were performed using cells from Thaw991 to assess the productivity of the cell bank (E6711, E6715 & E6718). All 3 QC attempts fell below the required specifications for productivity. A new PSS vial was therefore thawed (Thaw1000) for QC, this time with the addition of HFCS.
Impact Assessment: Since none of the QC analyses performed on cells from Thaw991 met the Product Specification, approval of the PSS was postponed by a few additional weeks. However, the timeline for project 2025‑29 was already behind schedule, so the impact was minimal. In addition, the cells from Thaw991 were only used for QC, and not for further production, so the PSS itself was not impacted by the lack of HFCS at thawing.
Root cause description: Why did the operator not use HFCS during thawing of QC test Because operator understood the instruction in Product specification to be only relevant for initial thawing. Why did the operator believe HFCS was only relevant for initial thawing Because the wording in the PS was not precise, and could be interpreted to mean either only after inital thawing or after every thawing. Why was the wording in the PS not precise Because this was the first time producing these cells for customer and wording was taken directly from technical documentation provided by customer. Product Specifications should not be up for interpretations - they must be precise, concrete and easily understood to ensure that production always complies with customer agreements, and is not person dependent. Root cause of this NC can therefore be considered a lack of precision in the customer agreement. To prevent this issue from happening again, the Clone Experience tracker in BizzMine has been updated to specify that clone Y.ruckeri O1b 23.1.1 should always (no matter the purpose, i.e QC, PSS/MCB etc.) be thawed with the addition of HFCS, to optimize production. As this non-conformance is specific to this clone and this customer, it is concluded that no further corrective actions are needed at this time.
Classification justification: This NC is not in comliance with the customer agreement, and is therefore considered high risk.

Non Conformance Title: Final product didnt meet product specification for SDS-PAGE
Investigation summary: The product acceptance criteria is written based on IgG requirements, which increases the risk of misclassifying normal IgM band patterns as deviations. The ~66 kDa band observed under non‑reducing conditions is consistent with a possible μ heavy‑chain dissociation event rather than a confirmed IgG impurity, as the structural properties of IgM—particularly its extensive disulfide‑linking—can make it sensitive to SDS conditions. Furthermore, the SDS‑PAGE method used is not optimized or validated for IgM, meaning method‑induced artefacts cannot be ruled out, and similar patterns have been repeatedly documented for IgM, indicating a systemic methodological limitation rather than a batch‑specific product issue. Additionally, the purity criterion (>90% under reducing conditions) is not appropriately defined for IgM, which includes multiple components beyond heavy and light chains, such as the J‑chain. Calculating purity by summing visible bands is poor analytical practice because intact light chain can artificially inflate the purity value and mask heavy-chain fragmentation, making the current specification unsuitable for IgM. B7541 is a very old batch. possibility of degredation of antibody during storage over time. Sample was made more than 5 years ago (january 2020). The purification method for IgM is not the most selective. It is known to co purify other large moplecules. Its a method based on size - where there are many cellular proteins in the range of 900-950 kDa. The cell viability in the inner chamber is usually between 10-40%, and because of the high amount of dead cells there is a high amount of large, intracellular proteins releaset in the harvest medium.
Impact Assessment: Method is not validated
Root cause description: This method is not oprimised or validated for testing IgMs, so we cannot rule out any method induced artefact bands, that occur due to sample preparation.
Classification justification: Product could not be released

Non Conformance Title: Infection in CL1000 unit during production
Investigation summary: An infection was detected in the outer chamber of CL1000 B9446 during H15, while the inner chamber and previous harvest (H14) showed no contamination. Review of handling steps found no procedural deviations, and only one of six CL1000 units was affected, indicating aseptic technique was maintained. Environmental monitoring, incubator and LAF assessments, and equipment checks all showed no CFUs. All media, supplements, and consumables were within expiry and showed no signs of contamination. The investigation found no identified source of contamination, and the event appears isolated to the outer chamber.
Impact Assessment: The contamination had no significant impact on the final product. The infection was confined to the outer chamber of one CL1000 unit at H15, while both H14 and the inner chamber were free of contamination. Production capacity was not compromised, as three full series of six CL1000 units were running in parallel, and large‑scale production continued as planned in the AcuSyst Maximizers. Only minimal product volume was lost, and the production timeline and delivery deadlines were fully maintained. No effect on product quality, availability, or process continuity was observed.
Root cause description: 5-whys root cause analysis 1. Why was there contamination in the outer chamber? Because the outer medium showed turbidity, color change, and odor, confirming microbial growth. 2. Why did microbial growqth occur in the outer chamber?Because a contaminant was introduced during handling or media exchange. 3. Why could a contaminant have been introduced during handling?Because the outer chamber requires manual medium exchange, and spillage or splash‑back can occur—especially with viscous RPMI + 1% serum, which increases the chance of accidental exposure. 4. Why is spillage or accidental contact more likely during media changes?Because when changing medium, the operator moves hands and gloves in and out of the LAF frequently, and lids (media bottle or CL1000 flask) may briefly come close to or contact non‑sterile surfaces. Although environmental monitoring showed no CFUs in the LAF, transient glove or lid contact cannot be ruled out. 5. Why could glove or lid contamination occur even when environmental monitoring is clean?Because glove integrity may be compromised during repeated handling, and momentary contact events (e.g., bumping the glove, touching flask edges, or accidental touching of unsterile surfaces) are possible but not captured by routine EM. Root Cause (Most Probable)    Unintentional contamination during manual handling or media exchange, likely related to glove contact, lid contact, or spillage during medium pouring. Environmental and equipment checks were negative, and the event was limited to one out of six parallel CL1000 units, indicating that the contamination was an isolated handling-related incident, not a systemic aseptic failure. Contributing Factors   Higher viscosity of serum‑containing      RPMI increases spillage risk.    High frequency of hand      movements during CL1000 handling increases chance of glove contact.    Manual, open handling steps      (lid removal, pouring) inherently carry risk.    Source contamination from media      cannot be fully excluded, though no evidence supports it.     Exclusions (Not Root Cause)            Inner chamber contamination      (none found)    Harvest      14 contamination (none detected)    Incubator or LAF contamination      (EM negative)    Equipment      malfunction (none observed)    Expired or compromised      media/consumables (all within expiry)
Classification justification: This is classified as a high risk, as infection in production unit is a risk to product quality and cleanroom integrity
